draft.introduction: Mozi’s doctrine of Universal Love (jian ai) argues that impartial concern for all people, rather than Confucian graded love based on kinship, is the solution to social disorder. It is the most radical ethical proposal in classical Chinese thought.

translation.text: The sage, having a concern for the order of the world, examines the source of disorder. Whence does it arise? It arises from people not loving one another. If everyone in the world loved others as they love themselves, would any person harm another? Therefore the principle of universal love is the way to order the world.
translation.original_text: 聖人以治天下為事者也。必知亂之所自起

translator_notes.note: Mencius attacked Mozi’s universal love as denying the special obligations of filial piety—“to have no father.” This debate between impartial and graded love anticipates the utilitarian-deontological divide in Western ethics. Mozi’s position is closer to Christian agape or Buddhist metta than to Confucian ren.
